texts.orig: Recognized as a ' critical material ' [1], lithium's primary applications have been lithium-ion batteries (35%), ceramics and glass (32%), lubricating greases (9%), air treatments (5%), continuous casting mold fl ux powders (5%), and polymer production (4%) [2]. Among these, the extensive use of lithium-ion batteries, which represent the largest potential growth area for lithium compounds in global end-use markets, has signi fi cantly increased the consumption of lithium resources in recent years [2]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although lithium resources are abundant in the salt lakes located in West China, the majority of these resources have a high Mg/Li ratio, which is problematic because traditional precipitation methods are unsuitable for lithium recovery from this type of brine. In our previous work, constant-current electrodialysis (ED) was applied to comprehensively investigate the e ff ects of operating conditions on the Li + /Mg 2+ separation, however, the experimental study considering the feed characteristic diversity and the theoretical analysis of the ionic transfer process considering the feed composition complexity were remained to be perfected. This work focused on resolving the remained questions therein. Initially, we determined the ion-exchange isotherm of the CSO membrane. The selective a ffi nity towards divalent cation was validated, which laid the foundation for the development of the electric double layer (EDL). Then, we investigated the e ff ects of applied voltage on the separation performance and optimized the power mode. The constant-voltage was veri fi ed to be a superior power mode compared to the constant-current applied in our previous work. Thereafter, the feed solutions characterized by di ff erent Mg/Li ratios, Na/Li ratios, and sulfate concentrations were treated by constantvoltage ED, wherein the partitioning principle was further explained via a thermodynamic analysis of the aqueous species distribution of ions. The results showed that in a high-salinity aqueous system, mass transfer was signi fi cantly a ff ected by the complexity of the ions ' existing forms, which notably determined the steric hindrance and charge e ff ect. As a speci fi c transfer phenomenon, we found that sulfate ions provided large bene fi t to lithium recovery in the salt lake brine system. A natural brine experiment also showed that lithium recovery can be e ff ectively achieved by ED. These observations indicated that ED has a wide adaptability for lithium recovery from brines with di ff erent feed characteristics.

texts.orig: noticed that the transfer behavior of sulfate and chloride was unusual due to the existence of cation -sulfate complexes, but the e ff ect of anion species on cation transfer behavior was not considered. In these current studies, separations were achieved with relatively simple electrolyte-solution systems, which have low ionic strengths and in which each ionic species has similar ionic activity. Thus, the general separation principle suggested in these studies may not be suitable for a salt lake brine system, as the existing forms of ion species in these aqueous systems with a high concentration of electrolytes are complex. Additionally, the brine system has an extremely strong total ionic strength, and thus, the ionic interactions and charge e ff ects are strong. The concentration of co-existing ions, such as sodium, potassium, and sulfate, di ff er considerably in di ff erent types of brine, which may in fl uence the applicability of ED for lithium recovery. In our previous study [18], it was shown that the ion-fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ can be e ff ectively achieved by ED from a binary feed solution with a high Mg/ Li ratio. Nevertheless, the experimental study considering the feed characteristic diversity of brines was remained to be further perfected. In particular, the e ff ect of the anion species on separation performance has not yet been discussed. And the mass transfer considering the composition complexity of brine system should be further explained.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, constant-voltage ED using a batch mode of operation was performed to recover lithium from brines with di ff erent feed characteristics. As derived from our previous study, the power mode was optimized. In addition, the feed solutions characterized by di ff erent Mg/Li ratios, Na/Li ratios, and sulfate concentrations were treated by constant-voltage ED, wherein the partitioning principle was further explained via a thermodynamic analysis of the aqueous species distribution of ions and the ion-exchange membrane's properties. Then, a typical natural brine with a high Mg/Li ratio was treated to validate the applicability of lithium recovery via ED.

texts.orig: 2.1. Experimental setup
texts.text: 2.1. Experimental setup

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An Asahi Glass Co. DW-1 ED stack was used in a batch recycling setup comprising 40 repeating cells, each consisting of a cation exchange membrane (Asahi Glass Selemion CSO) and an anion exchange membrane (Asahi Glass Selemion ASA). Each membrane had dimensions of 18×55 cm 2 with an e ff ective area of 0.0507 m 2 and electrical resistance of 2.0 -2.3 Ω -cm 2 . As shown in Fig. 1, the same setup as our previous study [18] was used, which consisted of four separated circuits for the dilute, concentrate, cathode rinsing, and anode rinsing tanks, each equipped with a separate centrifugal pump, fl ow-meter, manometer placed at the solution inlet of the cell, tank, and control valve. A 3 wt% NaCl solution in the pH range of 2.8 -3.0 was used as the cathode rinsing solution, and the pH was adjusted by adding HCl. Strong oxidizing substances could damage the selectivity of the membranes. Thus, a non-chloride-reducing 3 wt% Na2SO3 solution was used as the anode rinsing solution to avoid Cl2 production at the anode.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Ion-exchange isotherm
texts.text: 2.2. Ion-exchange isotherm

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The thermodynamic equilibrium of Li + /Mg 2+ was determined at the membrane -solution interface without the in fl uence of an electric fi eld. Binary electrolyte solutions containing a single co-ion species (Cl -) were adopted to investigate the a ffi nity for cation species of the CSO membrane. LiCl·H2O and MgCl2·6H2O were used to prepare binary equilibrium solutions with di ff erent monovalent counter-ion fractions. Each equilibrium solution had a total counter-ion concentration of 0.1 mol/L. Prior to the determination of ion-exchange isotherms, CSO cation-exchange membrane samples with dimensions of 2×2 cm 2 were pre-rinsed using deionized water and treated as follows: (1) 50 mL of LiCl-MgCl2 binary electrolyte solutions with di ff erent Li + /Mg 2+ ratio were used to equilibrate membrane samples at 25 °C for 24 h. (2) Each equilibrated membrane sample was leached in 50 mL of 1 mol/L HCl at 25 °C for 24 h to exchange all Li + /Mg 2+ ions with H + . The ionic concentrations of the equilibrium solutions insteps (1) and (2) were determined by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Spectro Arcos FHS12) to obtain the equilibrium composition of the solution and membrane phase.

texts.text: 2.3.1. The effect of applied voltage

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed solution was fi ltered to remove suspended solids before performing ED. Experiments were run in batch mode, and all of the streams were recirculated during each batch. The fl ow rate of the dilute stream was fi xed at 1.0 m 3 /h (corresponding to a linear feed velocity in the stack of 7.1 cm/s), whereas the fl ow rates of other streams were adjusted to eliminate trans-membrane pressure in the membrane stack. The temperature of the ED stack was controlled at 20 ± 1 °C by a heat exchanger. The applied voltage was kept constant during each experiment. Both the initial dilute stream and the initial concentrate stream had a volume of 25 L. Experiments were performed in unsteady state runs, during which both the dilute and concentrate fl ows were sampled and analyzed by ICP-OES for the ionic concentrations at certain points. The volumes of both the dilute and concentrate streams were also recorded simultaneously.

texts.orig: LiCl·H2O and MgCl2·6H2O were used to prepare the feed solution to determine the e ff ect of voltage on Li + /Mg 2+ separation experiments. Considering the diversity in lithium grades and the Mg/Li ratios of salt lake brines, two representative feed compositions were adopted, including both a low-lithium and high-lithium concentration system. For the low lithium concentration system, the feed Mg/Li mass ratio was set to 150 and the feed concentration of Li + was set to 0.15 g/L to account for the worst lithium grade possible in natural brine. Voltages of 2.0 -6.0 V were used in these experiments. For the high lithium concentration system, the feed Mg/Li mass ratio was set to 18.9 and the feed concentration of Li + was set to 4.5 g/L. The feed composition is similar to that of East-Taijiner old brine, which is a brine with one of the highest lithium concentrations. Voltages of 12 -28 V were used in these experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although salt lake brines mainly consist of monovalent chloride ions, signi fi cant amounts of divalent sulfate ions are present in some salt lakes. Classi fi ed by anion species, there are sulfate-type brines (such as Yiliping salt lake with a sulfate concentration of 29 g/L) and chloride-type brines around the world. To investigate the feasibility of ED for lithium recovery from di ff erent types of brine, experiments with feed sulfate concentrations of 0 and 32 g/L were performed. LiCl·H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, and MgSO4·7H2O were used to prepare the feed solutions. Constant voltage ED was performed according to the conditions described in Section 2.3.1. Eq. (3) was used to calculate Li + recovery from each batch to determine the e ff ect of sulfate ions on lithium transport.

texts.orig: The ion-exchange isotherm of a CSO membrane at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 2. As shown, divalent counter-ions were preferentially adsorbed by the CSO cation-exchange membrane. It was only when the fraction of monovalent counter-ions in the binary electrolyte solution system became undeniably dominant (close to 1.0) that the adsorption ratio for monovalent ions increased signi fi cantly. It has been reported that the ions that are better retained in the ion-exchange membrane are transported more slowly [27], which laid the foundation for the development of the electric double layer (EDL).

texts.orig: be taken into account when considering transfer characteristics. Particularly for divalent counter-ions, ion pairs with co-ions form more easily. With regard to magnesium species, MgCl + has a larger radius than hydrated free Mg 2+ ions, and thus, is thought to be more easily sterically hindered. Meanwhile, the MgCl + ion pair has a lower positive charge density compared to hydrated free Mg 2+ ions, which results in the weakening of the electro-migration of magnesium species. The thermodynamic speciation software Visual MINTEQ V3.0 was used to calculate the species distribution of each ion species. As shown in Fig. 6, the percentage of hydrated free magnesium ions decreased remarkably as the Mg/Li ratio of the feed increased, and the percentage of the MgCl + ion pair increased as the Mg/Li ratio of the feed increased. However, hydrated lithium ions always were the dominant proportion of lithium species in the studied Mg/Li mass ratio range. These variations caused the permselectivity of Li + /Mg 2+ to increase when the feed Mg/Li ratio increased. On the other hand, the attraction arising from the Donnan e ff ect towards divalent counter-ions was stronger than that towards monovalent counter-ions. As the negatively fi xed charge density within the CEM decreased with an increasing concentration of divalent counter-ions due to their enhanced shielding e ff ect, the Donnan e ff ect was weakened. Hence, the variation of the Donnan e ff ect versus divalent counter-ion concentrations negatively a ff ected the permselectivity of Li + /Mg 2+ when the Mg/Li ratio of the feed increased. As a consequence, a relatively high feed Mg/Li ratio was bene fi cial to the selectivity of Li + /Mg 2+ , as shown in Fig. 5. The permselectivity was simultaneously governed by the variation in the free hydrated form proportions versus the divalent counter-ion concentration and the variation in the Donnan e ff ect versus the divalent counter-ion concentration.

texts.orig: tions shown in Fig. 9. Of further note is that the proportion of free chloride ions increased from 63.33% to 67.96% as 32 g/L sulfate was added to the feed solution. This variation enhanced the transport of cations, explained as follows. The permselectivity of anions is determined by their charge e ff ect and steric hindrance [30,31]. Due to the a ffi nity of the anion-exchange membrane (AEM) for divalent anions, sulfate ions are more strongly retained within the AEM, resulting in a slower transport of sulfate ions. On the other hand, chloride ions are able to more easily permeate the AEM due to the lower steric hindrance e ff ect. In principle, a certain number of anions permeating through an AEM gives the same electric equivalent as cations permeating through a CEM; this correlation is determined by the electrical neutrality of the solution. Therefore, the increasing proportion of monovalent anions (Cl -) in the feed solution would enhance the transport of anions as well as that of cations. As a consequence, in high-salinity aqueous systems, the presence of sulfate negatively a ff ected the transport of lithium ions.

texts.orig: 3.5. Natural brine process
texts.text: 3.5. Natural brine process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section 3.2, an applied voltage of 20 V was used for lithium recovery from East-Taijiner old brine. As shown in Table 5, the Mg/Li mass ratio was reduced to 2.07 after 3.5 h of batch recycling ED. This Mg/Li mass ratio can meet the demand of subsequent precipitation processes needed for lithium carbonate production. Additionally, Li + recovery can reach 90.5%, with the consumption of only 0.0045 kWh of electric energy per gram of lithium recovered. It is also worthwhile to note that almost all of the sulfate ions were stopped from migrating to the product stream. This e ff ect would also assist in the production of lithium carbonate. Compared to NF [13,14], ED exhibited superior lithium recovery, selectivity, and energy consumption for treating natural brine to recover lithium and was veri fi ed to be a feasible process to extract lithium from salt lake brines.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study aimed to verify the adaptability of ED with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes for recovering lithium from salt lake brines with di ff erent feed characteristics. The ion-exchange isotherm of a Selemion CSO membrane was initially determined, which veri fi ed that divalent counter-ions were preferentially adsorbed by the CSO cation-exchange membrane without the in fl uence of an electric fi eld. Then, constant voltage ED was performed to treat lithium-containing solutions with dominant quantities of magnesium. The experimental results showed that a relatively high applied voltage enhanced the selectivity and maintained higher lithium recovery. In addition, constant-voltage ED has been shown to be more cost-e ff ective compared to constant-current ED for lithium recovery. ED also exhibited superior permselectivity and a high rejection of sulfate when treating feed solutions with di ff erent Mg/Li ratios, Na/Li ratios, and sulfate con-
texts.text: This study aimed to verify the adaptability of ED with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes for recovering lithium from salt lake brines with di ff erent feed characteristics. The ion-exchange isotherm of a Selemion CSO membrane was initially determined, which veri fi ed that divalent counter-ions were preferentially adsorbed by the CSO cation-exchange membrane without the in fl uence of an electric fi eld. Then, constant voltage ED was performed to treat lithium-containing solutions with dominant quantities of magnesium. The experimental results showed that a relatively high applied voltage enhanced the selectivity and maintained higher lithium recovery. In addition, constant-voltage ED has been shown to be more cost-e ff ective compared to constant-current ED for lithium recovery. texts.orig: Moreover, mass transfer through the CEM was discussed throughout via a thermodynamic analysis of the aqueous species distribution of ions. In a high salinity aqueous system, the mass transfer was signi fi cantly a ff ected by the complexity of the ion species ' existing forms, which notably a ff ected the steric hindrance and charge e ff ect. Hence, the mass transfer through the ion-exchange membrane of each ion species was determined by its dominant existing form.
